title: Re: how do u guys beat bosses?
text:
Just dodged and beat them down. Depending on the boss I might be a bit more passive and evasive earlier in the fight to initially learn its moveset for like 30s to a minute before being more aggressive. I may dial it back briefly if I see any major behavioral/attack pattern changes when their health gets low, too.

Other than that just don't be reckless and greedy for dmg, don't tunnel vision and properly observe their attacks. Beat most of them first try, about 90% or so of the bosses. And of course, using proper tactics.

A few examples of tactical awareness:

- Not using clones at the start of a fight so you can race to not have them during the hardest part. Who are you even DPS racing against anyways? Yourself? Save clones until the last 40% health or so of the fight to help secure victory for most fights rather than wasting during the easiest part. This means to make sure you reserve enough mana for its use, though.

- Transformations can be used multiple times a fight depending on the transformation, but some may only likely get used once. That once should also be at the end of a fight in most cases, but not always. This is doubling up on the clone tactic. Suddenly near end of fight and low health? Transform to get a full free healthbar to avoid their deadly attack if decoy isn't ready or you think their current attack pattern is too dangerous to heal during. You can revert after dealing your free dmg in that form or when you know it is safe moment to get that heal off. If you did your clone tactic already and the boss is still barely alive it might be the surefire key to winning rather than risking a heal by continuing to press maximum offensive focus. An example of where you might transform earlier in a fight is the laser boss in the prison that warps around at the start. The fire form has a dash skill that lets you rapidly chase and deal free dmg, forcing its teleport, rinse and repeat until it gets anchored in place in the next phase. You can use those consumable items to recover the cooldown on it to use it multiple times for particularly hard fights if need be.

- Vessels can be used to stunningly unfair advantage on bosses. The fan is a well known one for certain bosses you will find out later in the game, like Erlang, the guy with the hand head, etc. The Bell is often neglected but among the most powerful. It's not the dmg reduction portion that is strong, though it is pretty nice too. It's the knock down interrupt that can instantly end most boss attacks in the game and knock them down allowing you to negate their big dangerous attacks or wombo-combo stunlock them combined with other knock downs from multi-focus heavy attacks and spirits. Combined with the spinning staff method to rapidly recharge all three (focus for heavy attacks & Qi for spirits + vessels) you can get some pretty absurd chained stuns off on a boss. Plus, some bosses it trivializes and people forget about it's wind removal effect like the famously hated Dear boss with its tornadoes that the bell actually outright disables but people constantly forget. The flame cloak can make one of the harder bosses, Bishui, which people often force to the ice form in 2nd area to make easier than fire and still find the boss hard... when in reality, you can keep it in the 1st area and just use the flame cloak to massively negate its dmg while getting free focus and spam the cloak constantly with the recharge staff spin method. Suddenly, a hard boss is trivialized.

- Some bosses like Yin Tiger expect different tactics/approaches and will punish you if you are completely inflexible and unwilling to learn / adapt.

- Others like the floating dragon in early chapter 3 or the giant chicken optional boss get a lot of hate for their "hitboxes" being bad but the reality is the players are literally just missing them entirely. They're intentional designs make them harder to hit if you just blindly attack. The dragon is a small floating kind of dot from the front, and a lot of Wukong's attacks will be too low and horizontal to hit. Alternatively, if you get to the side it's more like a horizontal line so you need to use vertical attacks like the smash attack. As another solution you can focus on thrust attacks to directly aim for the head. The same goes for the chicken with its deceptive visual illusion of range due to size and skinny angled legs. It frequently steps back causing many attacks to whiff for those not paying attention, especially with its leg shape and the sizing throwing sense of distance off. The camera only targeting the head is the icing on the situation. Instead, either opt for horizontal attacks while making sure you are close enough, or very long vertical attacks to reliably hit the head (aka focused smash, preferably), or simply use thrust stance. Alternatively, attack from the side where a lot of it's design tricks are negated.

- Immobilize is often blown for extra dmg, but it's real trick is to let you get very easy free staff spin hits in so you can rapidly recharge focus, spirits, and vessels all at once for more exploitation in combat, while still getting pretty adequate dmg from the staff spin.

- Decoy is super overpowered, but not everyone recognizes it or uses it properly. Sadly, it completely surpasses deflect, plus 3-4 focus heavy attacks already accomplish what deflect can with the right perks... It is very short CD compared to most abilities, very versatile, can be used to perform multiple feats at once, but must be properly considered for its mana usage which is over time and not a one cast cost. Often, you see people wasting it for the super tiny micro flinch effect rather than getting heavily boosted smash attacks, spirit attacks with tons of free critical chance added, buffing, summoning clones, healing, or multiple actions, not to mention a free get out of jail from hard to avoid attack / low health card.

- A bit of observation will make it clear that pillar stance is slower and weaker than the other two most of the time, unless you are specifically using it to avoid an attack that wont hit you while on it because you're bad at that enemy, which is unnecessary but a fine crutch for those who need it. Thrust can be used with charge attack spam for safe-ish hit and run tactics. However, the real prime stance is smash which has a fast animation (much faster than pillar, slightly slower than thrust), fast charge time, the highest scaling damage of all stances (yes, they're not equal on any of these points mentioned), good tracking, and a huge dmg reduction /tenacity boosting perk.

To be clear, the ironbound resolve skill's passive boost on specifically charged heavy attacks is so powerful that you can straight face tank trade bosses and 1) It interrupts almost all boss attack follow-ups and staggers them 2) It grants very high tenacity (aka interrupt resistance) 3) the dmg reduction is very very high, while you still get to nuke bosses 4) Only smash stance gets it. Once you learn this factor, and particularly when using Pilgrim / Centipede sets you'll find you 1-focus (sometimes 2-focus) all trash mobs instantly killing them and can nuke bosses very fast while focusing on an evasive observational playstyle. While you're charging your attacks you are ready to react/dodge and are often at a distance, aka not in range to be hit by most types of attacks, unlike normal attack patterns so it is actually loosely adopting Thrust's benefits but you get to keep your mobility, and you would be surprised how much can be avoided by simply running without a dodge. If you need to dodge the final perk in the smash tree lets you keep all charging focus on a perfect dodge. Factor in that the varied combos do relatively poor overall DPS compared to charged smash attacks, put you at risk being so close constantly and locked in animation during enemy attack, don't get some of the best buff effects in the game, and even the varied combo heavy attacks have weaker dmg/stagger/knock down ability and no dmg reduction mean you ultimately learn that just using charging focus and smash attacks for most of the game is overwhelmingly more effective, followed by thrust charging as an alternative weaker solution.

- Spirit choice, especially with staff spin recharging method, can also be hugely impactful depending on approach/boss but is often neglected until they learn its value at the final (optional) super boss, aka the literal end of the game... usually because some YouTube video probably taught them the method they didn't realize.

This is just to list a few tactical solutions and observations that often go neglected.

Sekiro aside, most of FROM's other games are also very tactical approach oriented which would trivialize a lot of their difficulty but often gets neglected.

So, for me, it wasn't an issue but it depends on who you ask, naturally. Some are more aware and prone to adapting than others, while others will use the same basic approach no matter what from start to end until, or at least until forced to change due to total non-progress.

While I've not had issues with the bosses, I do think YouTube makes sense for anyone stuck on a particular boss to see if their approach is wrong (possibly their approach to combat in the entire game might be wrong and they can learn from it), but I don't think it is likely necessary for most people. This game is a bit harder than much of FROM's games, but somewhere comparable to Sekiro, a bit harder perhaps. It's not that bad.
label: analytical
notes: Extremely long, detailed tactical breakdown; explains how and why game mechanics work; includes comparisons to FROM games

title: Re: how do u guys beat bosses?
text:
One of the first upgrades you can get restores your health for each focus point you use. Another early upgrade lets you do counter attacks with the light into heavy follow-up attack so long as you have at least 1 focus point, making you invincible, does excellent damage, can stagger enemies and interrupt them, as well as refunding the focus point if you time it right. That's your bread and butter as early as having 2 skill points in the beginning of chapter 1.

Spirits can stagger bosses, deal great damage, are not on a cooldown timer but instead on a meter you fill by doing damage. Those can be used to interrupt or pressure bosses. There are other stuff you can do as well as you progress.

title: Re: how do u guys beat bosses?
text:
You've only played this game then.

Dark Souls 1-3 and Elden Ring all can literally 1-shot all the bosses, have large numbers of skills that uber nuke and stunlock bosses, has far more accessible absurd EHP (effective health points, aka tanking ability), and more cheeses then perhaps any other game. Don't even get me started on the basic mobs that can almost all universally be stunlocked even at level 1 with a dagger until they die.

I do like those games, hence my beating them all at level 1 +0 upgrades multiple NG+s, etc. but you're being ridiculous with your claim. The only souls-like games that are in the same difficulty category are going to be Sekiro, and of course Ys at higher difficulties smashes them both.


You're stuck on the bald monk? Seriously?

I jest, it's a rite of passage. He's actually one of the harder bosses in the game, because his damage is very high for how early you encounter him compared to most bosses, you aren't experienced with the game yet, and your lack of abilities/fight options yet.

He can be beaten that early, definitely. Took me 3 tries as one of the bosses I actually died against, but took some people literal hours (somehow?) and then they found many others much easier after.

A tip if you didn't already find it, but there is a fire transformation from a nearby boss from the shrine facing towards the bald boss west of it. That form has its own separate full health bar and you can spam the dash move for avoiding dmg and higher dps than the basic attacks.

You can tagger the boss with either 2 focus attacks or the varied combos heavy attacks.

Alternatively, honestly if you just are annoyed with that boss go past it and come back later when you are a lot stronger. All you have to do is before you ring the 3rd bell unlocking (warping you) the secret area is to beat this boss. If not mistaken, you might be able to do it at any point of the game, but not 100% so I recommend just doing it before chapter 1's final boss the giant flaming bear > beat bald monk > ring 3rd bell and go to hidden temple and beat gold bald monk > then beat flaming bear.

The bald monk, Wandering Wright, is the strongest spirit in the game btw, but not early on. Early it's just okay. It's real strength comes as you get Cloud Step upgraded to give the huge crit chance boost (up to +50% on next single hit attack) and when also used with other buffs like Pigrim Set, etc. attack dmg boosts. It can nuke bosses and 1-shot them even. Early on while it is still not that strong it can be great at doing a nice boost of dmg and interrupting/knocking down bosses in the middle of their attacks. It can also break some cracked walls unlocking secrets...

The gold monk is much easier btw, almost impossible to lose to due to a special armor you get for that boss that lets you infinitely heal.

title: Re: how do u guys beat bosses?
text:
I know this is a troll topic , but for a serious answer to anyone finding the game hard.

The main key to this game seems to be understanding that there is no input queue, and more importantly, that you cannot cancel out of animations.

So for example in most games you would mash light attack with mechanics like this game has, and then when you see an attack you want to dodge, simply stop mashing attacking and press the dodge button with the direction you want to dodge. In most games the attack animation would be cancelled and you would start dodging. In this game you will get pummeled doing this as your light attack animation has to finish before you can dodge.

Hence in this game you should only be pressing light attack again (or whatever input you want) as the animation for the prior button is ending, otherwise if you see the need to dodge you will not be able to and you will find the game much harder then it actually is. Personally i'd prefer them to allow animation cancelling, but the game wasn't designed with that in mind and likely would trivialize a lot of it, as it was designed around these animation locks.
label: analytical
notes: Explains core game design mechanics (input queue, animation canceling) and why they affect difficulty; focus is on understanding how the game works

Yeah, the second run victory lap is a really crazy shift. It starts picking up again in later NG+s either forcing basically no dmg runs or using heavy dmg reduction since armor, alone is not enough... but that scaling is really quite extreme.

Some people seem to like it, though I'm not a fan since it removes a lot of the fun. Wish it scaled more like Code Vein's.

I kind of wish it had a NG+ option that reset everything but level and spirits, so you can try more build variety on new runs but avoid OP weapons/armor/OP healings.

Part of the issue, though, also could be you're just way better at the game and hit a clicking point where you just went boss mode. Could be fun to see how a fresh NG run works out with the knowledge/experience, but... having to do 3 runs just to get everything might burn you out before then... >.<

title: Re: how do u guys beat bosses?
text:
I never tested on this boss, but I assume like most bosses lightning attacks are genuine lightning dmg. So you can mitigate the threat by going to the village, if you did the quest to unlock it during this chapter, and speak to Alchemist to reallocate your pills into lightning resistance. This coupled with armor (if you want a specific armor upgrade it at Yin Tiger in same village) and any buffs can dramatically reduce damage.

First, having around 300~350 defense is approximately 50% dmg reduction. Having 100 Lightning is 50% reduction for that, or coupled with defense 75% reduction total. Hint, not worth pushing past that. At around 1.5k defense is another 50% reduction, or aka 75% reduction vs no reduction while 1000 lightning (the only element this can be achieved on with Golden Lining transformation) does the same but scales a bit faster as you can see.

Now, realistically, you're not going to hit 100% lightning, but you might be able to get somewhat close or at least 50%.

So with these two reductions, you can throw some lightning/debuff resistance pills and further thwart the lightning status effect which causes you to take more dmg thus decreasing dmg in three ways. You can further reducing dmg with the Vessel bell from chapter 2's secret area (go get it if you don't have it, it also does a very strong stun on bosses interrupting their attacks/knock down). Ring of Fire can further reduce dmg if you want, and some curios/pellets do the same though I forget what ones you have by that point in NG.

However, all of the above isn't super needed with this other tip.

If you haven't gotten it there is a slightly hidden quest in chapter 3 that unlocks a new magic called Spell Binder. This is a physical dmg oriented buff that disables magic/vessels/spirits in exchange for a large offensive physical staff oriented boon. It scales harder based off your max mana at the time of casting. The ability is useful for this boss because he is susceptible to charging smash attacks as they bypass his golden form avoiding being recoiled and break his shield.

Further, he only does his golden form again if you use the Immobilize spell iirc which you will not here. In addition, while you're charging your smash you can run around to avoid him then run up when ready to release and out trade him.

Now I advise to use the pill buff consumable that increases tenacity if you want to be a bit sloppy and just spam smash because this is one of the few bosses that has some attacks that can interrupt your 3-focus smashing attack. If you use tenacity buff consumables/pellets you just attack through his clashing with your skill's heavy dmg reduction on smash attacks (if you got the perk in the skill tree for smash) and out trade him. Rinse and repeat and he will die in like 20s, not a joke. Maybe 30-40 if you're a bit slower. Of course, I just tossed some attack buffs and crit buffs too so that helped, and you will have those in NG if you've been exploring and speaking to the alchemist NPC occasionally.

You can also use the spirit that grants on attack buff before you cast Spell Binder at the start of the fight to give you a nice head start.


I will warn you that spell binder is a bit of a trap later on though, but not many notice if they're not the analytical type it seems. The scaling falls off due to how it scales and by the time you start rolling around chapter 5 other buffs like Pilgrim Set/Centipede scale so hard while getting spells/spirits/vessels it makes Spell Binder really inefficient for bosses, and in NG+ it just becomes pointless no matter what armor you are using. Further, even if you don't use those armors in NG the spells when used properly will allow you to simply outperform spell binder dramatically. That said, spell binder can be convenient for clearing trash mobs a bit more efficiently in chapter 4/5 NG.
label: analytical
notes: Extremely detailed mechanical breakdown; explains damage reduction calculations, spell mechanics, and optimization strategies; focus is on understanding how the game functions at a technical level

title: Re: attracting young pawns?
text:
Throwing pollution packs at tribal villages can get you more pawns to sift through, Ideology has medical pods that can do age reversal (slowly, 1 year per cycle) and Anomaly has an age reversal ritual that can do bigger jumps but requires a victim to dump the years into.

Otherwise you would be looking mostly towards mods.
label: analytical
notes: Explains game mechanics and DLC features; provides solutions

title: Re: attracting young pawns?
text:
If you have a male colonist or a slave, you can extract ovum from females 50 and under and fertilize them for making a new generation of colonists.

Growth vats can accelerate and simplify this process as well as allow you to skip the baby stage of development. You will have slightly more sick or dead babies compared to a perfect natural birth(which is difficult or impossible at older ages anyways), but you get more chances because of extraction rates for ovum. It does require a lot of food to do this however.
label: analytical
notes: Explains game mechanics; details pros/cons of different approaches

title: Re: attracting young pawns?
text:
It's under "fertility procedures" research, and only requires basic herbal medicine as a minimum.

Yeah, it is a bit down the line of development, but you have time if it is a long term colony. Raising children is also quite difficult, as they take a lot of time, resources and provide little until they are older which takes ~3 ingame years normally. If you throw them in a vat the entire time it will be less than a year, but they will be far less developed.

Additionally, most oif my early base will be improvised and opportunistic. Often there are various structures that can be chesply and quickly repaired and repurposed. title: Re: New playthrough curiosity
text: Sometimes I do custom scenarios with some research already unlocked. Like for example a modified rich explorer start with the intent of making a coldest possible planet and then settling in the coldest part of the sea ice with two arctic explorers. As this would have been an impossible scenario with the normal starting technology I had to make hydroponics, biofuel refining, solar panels and battery already unlocked so I can have food, and enough starting materials to build the minimal amount of shelter and production/power generation to sustain the starter colony, and also warm clothes for the starting colonists so they can survive while they build the place. From there forward you had to make do with zero resources on the map, having to manage non-existent resources for a growing colony and figure out how to utilize heat most efficiently.

title: Re: Synthetic Man's Review!
text:
I think the "reviewer" content creator sounds awful, discriminative and somwhat out of touch on some topics.
But I do agree with with some of the gameplay stuff (I have listed that myself)

IO was part of the big gamespot scandal with "paid" reviews, where a game journalist was fired for not giving a high enough score to the game,
The vast majority of "professional" review sites, is more about marketing (so are many content creators as well)

It is much better to watch/play the game yourself and see all information, then judge it on that. In terms of success, then it boils down to if it is a financial (commercial) success, if it is liked by the broader gaming scene and if it is like by those that end up buying it (most that buy a game will be positive minded)

Commercially this game did under avarage and might never recoup its massive 200m development budget. With mere 3m sales after 3 weeks cross platforms.
On PC (steam) it has not been very popular within its scope, but it have been well recieved by those that end up buying it however.

Overall. This is a decent walking and action simulator with cinematics. It is obviously focused on a very very casual segment of the mainstream and a experience the story and feel engaged by very little interaction.

I don´t think characters are well done, the dialogues feel language algorithm made and overall story is mid at best. The combat is simplistic, boring and very easy. The segments where you basically just click to progress the linear scenes, could have just been a cinematic section instead..

What it does well is being inclusive (we talk about gamers, as in people that never held a controller before, would most likely be able to easily finish and enjoy this) to players and I feel like the gunplay/melee is decent as well, evem more so if you are a casual gamer.
label: analytical
notes: Detailed analysis of game mechanics, commercial performance, and audience targeting; explains how and why the game works; includes industry context

title: Re: Huge VAC wave ongoing
text:
Okay…so here's how VAC actually works. The best analog to describe it is antivirus software…only for cheats. When a piece of a cheat app's memory signature is hashed into the VAC blacklist, anyone who used it in the past…gets a VAC Ban instantly. The huge cheating problem through anomalous behavior in just gave Valve a large backlog to go into game recordings and find out what memory signatures don't match standard gameplay.
Source/2.0 Engine doesn't use video logging for that type of recording. It uses key and input logging along with how long you alt-tab for…stuff like that. Point is the recoding system logs what's being stored in RAM.
label: analytical
notes: Explains VAC system mechanics; technical breakdown of anti-cheat
